entry.title: Partner Function Replication Issue in C4C V2: Sales Area–Dependent Account Team Members Overwritten
entry.content:
We are currently replicating Business Partner data from SAP S/4HANA to SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) V2 via CPI. The requirement is to maintain partner functions exclusively in C4C, so we have disabled the Partner Function node in the CPI message mapping.

However, after this change, we observed unexpected behavior during replication:
with every Business Partner update from S/4HANA, only the Account Team Members that have a Sales Area dependency are being removed/overwritten, while other Account Team Members (without Sales Area dependency) remain unchanged.

Test Scenario:

  • A Business Partner exists in C4C with three Account Team Members:
    • Some with Sales Area dependency
    • Some without Sales Area dependency

  • A change is made in S/4HANA for this Business Partner
  • The replication to C4C is triggered again

Observed Result:

  • Account Team Members with Sales Area dependency are deleted/overwritten
  • Account Team Members without Sales Area dependency remain intact
  • This occurs even though the Partner Function node is not included in the payload

 

Has anyone encountered a similar behavior when replicating Business Partner data to C4C V2?
What would be the recommended approach to prevent the Sales Area–dependent Account Team Members from being overwritten during replication?

entry.title: From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This June
entry.content:
In the age of AI, the skills we build today will define how we work tomorrow. That’s why we’ve launched a monthly blog series that spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities. 

Building on my first blog, where I introduced you to our AI live sessions Beyond Hype (which are all about getting productive with SAP Business AI), I wanted to address some of your most asked questions.  

  1. How are agents audited and monitored, and is there a mechanism to review and approve or deny actions before execution, or do they operate with full autonomy?
    SAP handles the security of AI agentic workflows from the design phase onwards in the following ways: Guidelines and controls applied to the hosting environment, proper authorization checks, keeping humans in loop for critical decisions or actions, and by limiting actions to a predefined list. Only after human approval does the agent execute the transaction, respecting role-based access controls.
  1. Which large language model powers Joule?      
    SAP maintains partnerships with leading large language model providers and selects the best‑performing model for each specific Joule or agent use case based on rigorous benchmark testing. As a result, the underlying model may vary by scenario to ensure optimal quality, performance, and fit.
  1. Can you give an overview of the lines of businesses that are available in the Joule Early Adopter Care (EAC) Program?
    If you followed the announcements at Sapphire 2026, you've probably noticed that Joule is evolving rapidly, offering a new generation of experiences with Joule Work, Joule Desktop, and Joule Studio 2.0. Therefore, SAP will launch Early Adopter Care (EAC) programs that give selected customers early access to upcoming Joule innovations while working directly with SAP product teams. To answer your question: SAP S4/HANA Public and Private Cloud Edition, SAP SuccessFactors, and SAP Ariba will be part of the Joule EAC programs.

Want to dive deeper into the content? Rewatch the session and access the session material: 

  • All Things Business AI: Latest Insights, Updates & Live Demos (recording)  

Everything we covered in this blog so far, from agents, human-in-the-loop design, to early adopter programs, is part of a much larger shift in how SAP is building for the future. For the full picture of what's coming, the Innovation News Guide from SAP Sapphire 2026 lays it out clearly:

  • Joule Work redefines how users engage with SAP software. Instead of navigating individual applications and entering data across several screens, users will interact primarily with Joule by describing a desired business outcome and letting Joule orchestrate the right combination of workflows, data, and agents to get it done. Sound familiar? That's the same human-in-the-loop approach we walked through earlier in this blog. See it in action in the Interactive Value Journey.
  • The SAP Business AI Platform is a new foundation for building and deploying enterprise AI grounded in business context. SAP Business AI Platform now unifies SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Business AI into a single, governed environment. Explore how the agents and applications you build understand your business, not just your prompts, in this Interactive Value Journey.

The shifts we've just explored are moving fast and the best way to keep pace is to learn alongside the people building them. Sign up for the SAP AI Adoption and Learning Week, July 6-9: a week of live, expert-led sessions combining strategic insights with practical guidance. The virtual event is free of charge, no SAP Learning Hub subscription required, just an S-user ID to register for individual sessions.

Stay tuned for more, and happy learning! 🚀 

Further Resources: 

  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This May
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This March  
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This February 
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This January 
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This December 
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This November 
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This October

Joule Studio Joule #SAP Business AI Platform

📢 Upcoming SAP Training: Year-End Payroll Updates (Germany)
entry.content:
I would like to highlight an upcoming SAP training that may be relevant for colleagues working with SAP HCM Payroll Germany:

WDEHR4 - Informationen aus der Personalabrechnung DE zum Jahreswechsel 1.1.2027 | SAP Training

This 1-day course provides a concise, practical overview of key year-end payroll changes (2026–2027). It covers:

  • The latest legal and statutory changes affecting payroll
  • New SAP functionalities
  • Customizing and implementation approaches
  • Practical insights into day-to-day payroll operations

💡Important to note:

  • This course WDEHR4 is only available in German and only covers topics related to the year-end changes in Germany. It is offered only twice a year in December, directly by the Payroll Germany development team. There is no option to repeat this event or offer it as a CST.

👉This course best supports German customers and teams working directly with SAP HCM Payroll Germany who want to deepen their expertise. It does not apply to other country payroll versions.

The event also fosters a strong community exchange, allowing participants to learn from experts and connect with peers in the field.

If you are preparing for the upcoming payroll year-end, this training offers a valuable opportunity to stay up to date and ensure a smooth transition.

Best regards

Tatiana

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 17 - Schema evolution testing
Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 07 - Authorization annotations
Re: Managing Invoices with SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (now SAP Ariba Invoicing)
entry.content:
Hello @sdalal72,

Thanks for reaching out. I am trying to answer based on my understanding. Kindly confirm further.

1. Does SAP Business Network comes together with SAP Ariba Invoicing (CIM) or does it need separate license ? If no, PO needs to be sent from S/4 Hana public cloud to SBN, this would be commerce automation. would be extra cost for this setup ?
From our perspective SBN would be best option to create and submit Xrechnung for germany.

SAP Business Network (SBN) and SAP Ariba Invoicing (CIM) are distinct products with independent licenses.
PO from S/4HANA Public Cloud can be sent to SBN via Commerce Automation. Commerce Automation is also a separate license.

2. If supplier creates X-rechnung from SBN, do we still need SAP DRC to receive and fetch invoice in CIM ?

Let us assume that you mean digital invoice creation by Supplier in SBN with XRechnung. SBN can send invoice directly to CIM.
DRC is used for compliance reporting. If this compliance is needed, you may have DRC in flow.

If the supplier creates and submits an XRechnung (official, machine-readable XML-based electronic invoice standard in Germany) via SAP Business Network (Peppol integration on SBN), the invoice travels through the Peppol network → SBN. In this case, for the invoice to land in CIM, the flow still requires DRC to validate and map the XML before forwarding to CIM.
The standard documented flow for receiving Peppol invoices into CIM is: Peppol Network → SAP DRC (cloud edition) → SAP Ariba CIM → S/4HANA
SAP Ariba CIM can be configured to receive invoices directly from the Peppol network via SAP DRC (cloud edition), without involving SAP Business Network.

Also refer this comment above for details on DRC.

3. Does receiving invoicing from PEPPOL Network (via DRC) to CIM still need SBN Integration ? If yes, what will be the flow ? If using PEPPOL network, do suppliers need onboarding on PEPPOL just like SBN?

Kindly refer point 2 above. SAP Ariba CIM can be configured to receive invoices directly from the Peppol network via SAP DRC (cloud edition), without involving SAP Business Network.

For the Peppol → DRC → CIM flow, suppliers need to be registered on the Peppol network (i.e., have a Peppol participant ID). They do not need to be onboarded on SAP Business Network. This is different from the SBN onboarding process.
The buyer's Peppol participant ID must also be maintained in the relevant system so invoices are routed correctly.

4. If supplier sends XML (Xrechnung) via email, do we need SAP DRC to extract that data into CIM? If yes, how it can be fetched, because in this case we need SAP DRC and RPA both.

Normal PDF Invoice or Invoice received via Email can be processed by CIM without DRC.
For XML (Xrechnung) (assuming Peppol involved here), SAP DRC is required for processing XML (XRechnung) invoices received via email into CIM. RPA is not mandatory here as per standard flow.

5. Our customer has entilements for sap ariba invoicing and sap ariba invoicing inbound processing module but they are yet to provision it. will be provisioned new way (sap managed) or old way (customer managed). How to know for which scope entitlements are (4N6 or 7YH)?

Kindly refer these comments on the same blog for more details.

  • 7YH
  • SAP-managed Deployment
  • EU’s VAT in the Digital Age (ViDA) Initiative and SAP's Invoicing Solutions
  • 3187800 - Peppol integration for SAP Business Network
  • Peppol Integration for SAP Business Network | SAP Help Portal

Anna

entry.title: Re: I would like to expose 3 specific views from schema S...
entry.content:
Solved — use a Database User Group connection.

The key was which database user the connection uses. A connection authenticated with a Database Analysis user did not show the SYS schema in Import Remote Tables. But a connection authenticated with a Database User Group user does expose SYS there — so you can import the system views directly, with no helper/exposure views in the database.

Steps that worked for me:

  1. Create a Database User Group (System → Configuration → Database Access) and note its credentials (host, port, user, password). Its admin user (DWCDBGROUP#…#ADM) already has unfiltered read on SYS — e.g. SELECT COUNT(*) FROM SYS.OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES returns the full count — while it has no access to business data in spaces, which is exactly the least-privilege scope I wanted.
  2. Create a SAP HANA connection in my space using those Database User Group credentials.
  3. Data Builder → Import Remote Tables via that connection → the SYS schema is listed → import the views I needed (SYS.VIRTUAL_TABLES, SYS.OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES, SYS.REMOTE_SOURCES) as federated remote tables.
  4. Build my SQL/Graphical Views in Data Builder on top of those imported tables — done, no database-level objects required.

So the short answer: the SYS schema is reachable from Data Builder — connect via a Database User Group user, then Import Remote Tables. In my case OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES + VIRTUAL_TABLES + REMOTE_SOURCES gave me everything I needed to build a connection "where-used" / impact-analysis view.

(One open question if anyone knows: why the SYS schema is visible to a Database User Group connection but hidden to a Database Analysis user connection — I couldn't find this documented.)

entry.title: Re: Request for Mass Purchase Requisition Upload Provision
entry.content:
Dear @watertec,

In Public Cloud there is currently no standard mass upload template, Fiori app, or Migration Cockpit object for creating standard Purchase Requisitions in bulk. SAP confirms this as standard behavior, because Purchase Requisition is an internal transactional document and no app or migration object is available for mass PR creation

More details below:

SAP KBA 3411702 – Mass creation of Purchase Requisition

The Mass Changes to Purchase Requisitions app is not suitable for this requirement, because it is used only to change already existing PR items; it does not create new PRs.

More details below:

SAP Help – Mass Changes to Purchase Requisitions

The recommended solution is to build an Excel/CSV upload or integration layer and create the PRs through the released API API_PURCHASEREQUISITION_2 Purchase Requisition OData V4. This API supports creating purchase requisitions from an external source system.

More details below:

SAP Help – Purchase Requisition – OData V4

SAP KBA 3350690 - Create Purchase Requisition and Purchase Order via template

Based on the current SAP standard and public roadmap information this should be treated as a standard product gap if the requirement is specifically for an SAP-delivered PR upload template or Migration Cockpit object. In that case the requirement should be raised through SAP Customer Influence

More details below:

SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
SAP KBA 2676589 – How to Submit Feature Request for Data Migration, SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Bex report key figures header text is changing when t...
entry.content:
Hi nranjith,

This is indeed an interesting topic as BW queries where historically not designed with multi-language support in mind but got enhanced with this functionality. The issue you describe indicates that on one system the query might have been saved in a new language having empty texts for fields having custom descriptions in another language (see below) or by activating the default value flag.

To work with multiple languages in the BW modeling tool you need to know the following:

  1. RSADMIN Parameter QUERY_LANG_MODE
    There is the RSADMIN parameter which controls the behavior for queries used in different languages. While the system default value is '00', when working with multi language scenarios the parameter needs to be switched to '03':
    • QUERY_LANG_MODE=00: if a query is opened in a different language, element descriptions maintained in the original language appear as Empty Description. This can lead to unwanted text changes of the original languages. See below.
    • QUERY_LANG_MODE=03: if a query is opened in a different language, element descriptions maintained in the original language appear as input ready text field prefilled with a copy of the Default Value of the selected language.

  2. Regardless of the RSADMIN parameter the following applies:

    • Default Value
      • If a query is saved in any language with the Default Value description for a particular element, the default value will be displayed for ALL languages. That setting is language independent and overrides custom texts that have been maintained for that element in other languages.
    • Empty Description
      • Characteristics: if a query is saved in any language with Empty Description it has the same effect as the Default Value. So, empty text isn't accepted.
      • Key Figures and other Structure Elements: if a query is saved in any language with Empty Description it is applied in all languages.

I hope this information will help you to correctly set up your BW queries with multiple language support.

Best regards,
Bernhard

entry.title: Business Data Architecture: Platform Responsibility in a Multi-Platform Analytics Landscape
entry.content:
The problem: When ownership isn't explicit

In the previous post, we established how to define business meaning by separating what you measure (Metrics) from how you analyze it (Measures and Dimensions). When Finance defines "Revenue" as a metric with its measures (Actual, Budget, YTD, Variance), that business ownership is clear.

But one critical question remains: Where should these definitions be implemented?

Without explicit platform responsibility, organizations end up with Revenue calculated differently on each platform, duplicate ingestion pipelines, and no clear answer to "which platform should implement this?" when changes are needed.

This post shows how to make platform responsibility explicit - so business-owned metrics are implemented once on the right platform and shared safely across platform boundaries.

Four principles of platform responsibility

To assign implementation responsibility, you first need to understand what each platform is for - its analytical purpose, aligned to specific users and business decisions.

Platform purpose is about business responsibility, not technical architecture:

  • Which business domains does this platform serve?
  • Which users and decision scenarios depend on it?
  • What business processes does it primarily support?

Example: Platform Purpose

  • SAP Datasphere - ERP Analytics Platform: Owns all core business metrics from SAP S/4HANA (revenue, cost, margin, customer, product) and serves Finance, Sales, and Controlling users. Primary decision support: financial reporting, business performance analysis, and management KPIs.
  • Snowflake - Manufacturing & Supply Chain Platform: Owns operational metrics from external manufacturing execution systems (MES) (production efficiency, capacity utilization, cycle time, schedule adherence) and serves Operations, Plant Management, and Supply Chain users. Primary decision support: production planning, manufacturing optimization, and supply chain analytics.

Once platform purpose is clear, four operational principles define how platforms work together without creating fragmentation:

 

Cross-platform access

In practice: Revenue metrics owned in SAP Datasphere (ERP Analytics platform) are shared to Snowflake (Manufacturing platform) for profitability analysis by production line. The metric definition remains unchanged - only the analytical context differs.

Business meaning ownership

Ownership follows business responsibility, not data origin. The platform responsible for a business domain owns the metrics for that domain - even if data comes from other platforms.

In practice: Manufacturing Cost per Unit is owned on Snowflake (Manufacturing platform), even though cost data originates from SAP S/4HANA. The metric lives where the business knowledge lives.

Controlled reuse

This is where many multi-platform landscapes fail: teams start by consuming shared data, but end up redefining it "just slightly" to fit local needs, creating platform-specific metric variants.

Shared data must preserve shared meaning. Consumption is allowed, redefinition is not.

In practice: Customer master data is ingested into SAP Datasphere from SAP S/4HANA and exposed as a Customer Dimension data product. Snowflake and Power BI consume this shared dimension without creating their own customer hierarchies or customer segmentation logic.

Single-source ingestion

In practice: SAP S/4HANA Financial Actuals are ingested once into SAP Datasphere. Snowflake consumes this through Datasphere's sharing layer, ensuring both platforms work from the same data version with a single extraction pipeline - reducing complexity, ensuring consistency, and making lineage traceable. Before this, separate ingestion into both platforms created version conflicts and maintenance overhead.

What this prevents

These four principles aren't theoretical. They prevent real failure patterns that emerge when platform responsibility isn't explicit:

 

Common concerns

Organizations increasingly run analytics across heterogeneous platforms - SAP Datasphere, Snowflake, Databricks, Microsoft Fabric, and Power BI often coexist. This gives flexibility but increases fragmentation risk. Platform responsibility enables flexibility without fragmentation by allowing different platforms to serve different purposes while maintaining consistent business meaning.

The circular flow concern

The most common reaction to this approach is: "Won't this create circular data flows?"

Consider this scenario: SAP S/4HANA data is ingested into SAP Datasphere. That data is shared to Snowflake where Manufacturing Cost per Unit is defined (because Snowflake owns the Manufacturing domain). Now Finance wants to use that metric in management reporting - which runs on SAP Datasphere. The data flows back from Snowflake to Datasphere.

This looks like spaghetti. SAP data goes to Datasphere, then to Snowflake, then back to Datasphere.

But this is the wrong way to think about it.

The data doesn't move in circles. What moves is value at different stages of transformation:

  • SAP Datasphere ingests raw transactional data from SAP S/4HANA
  • Snowflake receives that raw data and transforms it into a business metric (Manufacturing Cost per Unit) - adding manufacturing domain knowledge
  • SAP Datasphere receives that finished metric for consumption in management reports

The lineage is clear: raw data → domain-specific transformation → analytical consumption. The fact that the analytical consumption happens on the same platform that ingested the raw data doesn't make it circular - it reflects that SAP Datasphere serves Finance users for management reporting.

This is similar to how microservices architecture works. Service A calls Service B, which calls Service C, which returns data to Service A. That's not circular dependency - it's composition. Each service adds value at its layer.

The alternative - duplicating ingestion or moving all domain logic to one platform - creates the fragmentation these principles prevent.

The key question isn't "does data flow between platforms" - it's "does the flow follow clear responsibility boundaries?" If yes, the architecture is sound. If ownership is confused or metrics are redefined mid-flow, that's when you have a problem.

The organizational challenge

While the technical implementation of platform responsibility is well understood in 2026, the organizational shift is often harder than the technical challenge. For most platform teams, explicitly owning business meaning and exposing it as data products represents a significant cultural change.

Success requires:

  • Clear accountability: Platform teams must accept ownership of data quality and business meaning for their domain
  • Cross-team coordination: Shared dimensions and metrics require agreement across platform boundaries
  • Sustained commitment: Building and maintaining data products is ongoing work, not a one-time project

Organizations that succeed treat this as an organizational transformation, not just a technical architecture exercise.

Up next

In this post, we focused on where business meaning should be implemented - making platform responsibility explicit. The next post explores how to implement it consistently:

Modeling Layers as a Shared Discipline Across Platforms

  • How to control where data is transformed - from source to analytics
  • Why the same modeling discipline must apply across SAP Datasphere, Snowflake, and other platforms

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 16 - Escaped names
Compass: Predictive Range Proposal "Smart Boundaries"
entry.content:
Introduction

For SAP Analytics Cloud, version 2026.12, we have integrated the capability of Smart Predict into compass to generate forecast of possible driver fluctuations with one single click. Customers on quarterly release will enjoy this benefit from QRC3 onwards. With the integration, users can automatically generate boundaries for driver fluctuations based on analysis of the historical data, without the hassle of technical setup.

One-Click-Trigger to insert predicted ranges for driver fluctuations

 

 

 

How Does it Work?

Smart Boundaries can be triggered by a new button on the drivers list, with two options for predicted results: Either as absolute numbers or as relative percentages. Once triggered, a time series analysis of the available historical data will begin and loading indicators will appear within the input fields for the min/max values, concluding by feeding the results directly into the input fields once ready. You can then proceed to run the scenario with the suggested boundaries, or fine-tune the results manually first, if preferred. A prediction process can be aborted any time before its conclusion by clicking on the same button again.

Choose between absolute or relative values (%) for min/max boundaries

Range of Historical Data Used for Prediction
All historical data from the version used in the scenario will be analysed, up to the cut-off date, which is the configured data filter for the simulation driver. E.g. if the driver filter is “Gross Revenue Q4 2026” and the scenario is based on the version “Actuals”, then all available data for gross revenue in that version up to the last period before Q4 2026 will be taken into consideration. 
Note that the minimum ratio of historical data to result would be around 1:1, but depending on factors such as seasonality or data quality, more data might be required to produce results. Therefore, the recommended amount of historical data would be two to three years to capture cycles and trend.

 

Use Cases & Benefits

Generally, this feature is beneficial in cases where assistance is required for modelling uncertainty, including the following:

Understanding the Corresponding Risk of Underlying Trend: If keen to explore the question of "what are the risks if things continue the way as before", a scenario can be run using Smart Boundaries suggestions for key driver performances without adjustments, to simulate the compound effects for uncertainties across key drivers resulting from the historical development.
For this approach, only Smart Boundaries results are used to model key driver uncertainties.

Pre-configure Risk Guide Rails: Smart Boundaries can also be used to pre-populate the value configuration with guide rails derived from historical data, which can be used as a starting point to model mitigation strategy. This could be also particularly useful in cases where IT is creating a compass simulation to be handed over to business end users for further simulation. Instead of creating a simulation artifact with empty configurations for driver min/max values, IT could already pre-fill the driver list with Smart Boundaries results.
For this approach, end users will utilize Smart Boundaries insights and build on top of those by modifying them.

Accelerated Decision Intelligence: Often, a single user will not have the complete knowledge of possible fluctuation for all key drivers spanning across different regions, departments or line of businesses. Most likely, the end users are either experts in their domains or involved in discussions pertaining to drivers in their responsibilities and could model the uncertainties for those, but not the rest, for which they would need to seek insights from other topic experts or regions/departments. For situations where quick simulations are crucial to judge the current risk exposure and mitigation options, this could mean losing precious time for time-consuming alignments. In such cases Smart Boundaries could assist to fill in the blanks by modelling the uncertainties for drivers where the current trend is to be assumed.
For this approach, end users will be simulating with a mixture of drivers configured by Smart Boundaries and manual configurations.

 

FAQ

Is there an active link between the suggested boundaries and the model data?
No. Once generated, the boundaries are just any other input entry. It neither changes nor updates itself, and can be used as guide rails to be overwritten with new insights, if desired. 

Can I choose which range of data to use for the prediction?
No. Smart Boundaries will take all available data within the version used in the scenario, right up to the last period before the time filter definition for the driver. For example, if the driver is filtered down to Q1 2027, then all prior data in that version up to Q4 2026 will be used for the boundary prediction.

How much historical data would I need?
The minimum ratio is 1:1, so if generating predictions for a driver filtered down to Q1 2027, you will need at least one quarter of historical data. And if it is filtered down to 2027, you will need a year of data. But depending on the variability of your data, sometimes more data might be required to capture the seasonality and trend before results can be produced. Therefore a good rule of thumb is to have at least around two to three years of historical data.  

 

Conclusion

Smart Boundaries brings the best of both worlds together by using time series forecast to model driver uncertainties in compass. Depending on use case and usage, it can reveal corresponding risk for underlying the development trend, serve as guide rail for mitigation simulation, or provide fluctuations inputs for drivers where another source of insight is not at hand.

entry.title: Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin: Modernize Your OpenUI5/SAPUI5 App in Hours, Not Weeks
entry.content:
Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin: Modernize Your OpenUI5/SAPUI5 App in Hours, Not Weeks

Today, we're delighted to unveil the UI5 Modernization Plugin for coding agents. It is a comprehensive Claude Code and Github Copilot compatible toolkit that automates the entire modernization journey for your OpenUI5/SAPUI5 applications.

The plugin developed by the UI5 Core team is part of the UI5 plugins for coding agents and turns what once took weeks of manual effort into a task completed in hours, with verification at every step.

The Problem: Modernization Is Painful

The UI5 framework has evolved significantly. Modern best practices call for strict ES module imports, async component loading, CSP-compliant code, and elimination of deprecated APIs. The UI5 linter reliably detects these issues — but fixing them still requires deep framework knowledge and careful manual work.

Consider what a typical modernization touches: manifest configuration, component lifecycle, module imports, deprecated API replacements, CSP compliance, and test infrastructure — often across dozens of files. Each area has nuances and edge cases that require extensive domain expertise, and one wrong move can introduce runtime regressions that no linter will catch.

This is exactly the kind of repetitive, rule-heavy transformation that coding agents handle well.

The Solution: Five Phases, Fully Orchestrated

Target: Manifest version 2.0.0 - Minimum framework version 1.136.0

It runs as a five-phase orchestrated workflow, with each phase building on the previous one:

Phase 1 — Mechanical Baseline: Runs the UI5 linter's built-in autofix and modernizes your test infrastructure (QUnit/OPA5) to the Test Starter concept.

Phase 2 — Foundation: Updates your manifest.json and Component.js, the two files that define your app's configuration, dependencies, and lifecycle.

Phase 3 — Module System & Globals: The heavy lifting. Eliminates all instances of global namespace access in JavaScript and XML files, converts pseudo modules to library imports, detects cyclic dependencies introduced by the new imports, and catches runtime-breaking patterns the linter cannot see.

Phase 4 — Deprecated APIs: Replaces deprecated controls, partially deprecated API signatures, HTML bootstrap parameters, native HTML in XML views, and more.

Phase 5 — CSP Compliance: Extracts any remaining unsafe inline scripts to external files.

Each phase creates a separate git commit, so you can review, revert, or cherry-pick any phase independently. After all phases have completed, the plugin generates a MODERNIZATION-REPORT.md summarizing pass/fail statistics along with a MODERNIZATION-ISSUES.md documenting anything it couldn't fix automatically.

Built Around the UI5 Linter

Instead of reinventing detection, the plugin leverages the findings of the UI5 linter, where every linter rule ID maps to a specialized fix skill:

Linter Rule Fix Skill

no-globals (JS)/fix-js-globals
no-globals (XML)/fix-xml-globals
no-pseudo-modules/fix-pseudo-modules
no-deprecated-apiRouted to 8+ specialized skills based on context
async-component-flags/fix-component-async
csp-unsafe-inline-script/fix-csp-compliance

After every fix, the linter is re-run for verification. Combined with runtime blind-spot detection, this feedback loop ensures nothing slips through.

Beyond the Linter: Catching Blind Spots

Some issues only manifest themselves at runtime and thus cannot be detected by the UI5 linter. The plugin includes dedicated detection for these:

  • Cyclic module dependencies introduced when converting globals to imports
  • QUnit 1.x assertion patterns that break under QUnit 2.x
  • Sinon mocking via global chains in tests that fails after namespace removal

These blind-spot detections separate a tool that "fixes linter errors" from one that produces code that actually works.

You're in Control: Three Verification Modes

At the start of a modernization run, the plugin asks which verification mode you prefer and then applies it consistently at every phase boundary:

Mode What happens at each gate

Full autonomousRuns your build + tests automatically. On failure, attempts to fix and retries (up to 3 times) before escalating.
Half autonomousRuns build + tests, reports results, and waits for your input before proceeding.
ManualPrints a summary of what changed and waits for you to verify externally.

This means you can let it run end-to-end on a portfolio of apps in parallel, or stay hands-on and review every phase. Your choice.

What to Expect

In internal testing across SAP applications of varying complexity, the plugin typically resolves 80-90% of linter findings automatically, with the remainder documented in the MODERNIZATION-ISSUES.md for a manual follow-up.

A mid-sized app (~50 files, 200+ linter errors) completes in roughly 20-40 minutes in full autonomous mode - including verification of each phase. The resulting git history gives you one clean commit per phase, making code review straightforward.

19 Specialized Skills — Use Individually or Together

While /modernize-ui5-app orchestrates the full workflow, every skill can also be invoked independently. Dealing with just one category of linter errors? Use the targeted skill:

/fix-component-async              # Component.js async issues
/fix-manifest-json                # manifest.json issues
/fix-js-globals                   # no-globals errors in JS files
/fix-xml-globals                  # no-globals errors in XML views/fragments
/fix-pseudo-modules               # no-pseudo-modules, no-implicit-globals
/fix-linter-blind-spots           # Runtime patterns linter misses
/fix-cyclic-deps                  # Circular sap.ui.define dependencies
/fix-bootstrap-params             # HTML bootstrap parameters
/fix-library-init                 # Library.init() apiVersion
/fix-control-renderer             # Control renderer issues
/fix-deprecated-controls          # Deprecated controls, classes, interfaces
/fix-fiori-elements-extensions    # Fiori Elements V2 controller extensions
/fix-partially-deprecated-apis    # Partially deprecated API calls
/fix-table-row-mode               # Deprecated Table row properties
/fix-xml-native-html              # Native HTML/SVG in XML views
/fix-csp-compliance               # Unsafe inline scripts
/modernize-test-starter           # Test Starter modernization
/modernize-flp-sandbox            # Dedicated FLP Sandbox modernization skill

This makes the plugin useful not just for full modernizations but for incremental cleanup work on any UI5 project.

Getting Started

Prerequisites

  • Node.js v20.11.0, v22.0.0 or higher
  • An OpenUI5/SAPUI5 app with ui5.yaml in the project root
  • A clean git working directory (the plugin commits per phase)

Installation

Claude Code:

claude plugin install ui5-modernization@claude-plugins-official

GitHub Copilot:

Install via the VS Code extension marketplace or add the plugin to your Copilot agent configuration.

Run It

Navigate to your UI5 project directory and invoke:

/modernize-ui5-app

The plugin will check your prerequisites, ask for your verification mode preference, and begin the five-phase workflow automatically.

Learnings

Working on this plugin reinforced a key insight: modernization isn't just about replacing one API with another. It's about understanding how changes cascade.

For example, converting sap.ui.getCore().byId("myTable") to a module import seems straightforward - but the new import can create a cyclic dependency that silently breaks at runtime. The fix for one linter error introduces a failure no static analysis will catch.

We've encoded hundreds of these nuances learned from real modernization projects into the plugin's skill definitions. The result is a tool that doesn't just follow rules but understands how the framework's modules, lifecycle, and runtime interact.

Try It Today

The UI5 Modernization plugin is available now in the official Claude marketplace and the Awesome Copilot marketplace. Whether you have a single app to modernize or a portfolio of legacy applications to modernize, this plugin can dramatically reduce the effort and risk involved.

Start with one app. Run /modernize-ui5-app. Review the commits. We think you'll be impressed by what comes out.

The UI5 Modernization Plugin is part of the UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents ecosystem. For feedback and issues, reach out via the plugin's repository or the UI5 community channels.

entry.title: BP_DATAREPL_FND/006 error when replicating Business Partner from SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 to SAP S/
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am working on the integration scenario 6KO - Opportunity to Order with SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 and I am facing an issue with Business Partner replication from SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Scenario:
SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 → SAP Cloud Integration → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Issue:
When a Business Partner is modified in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 and the replication is triggered back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the Message Monitoring in S/4HANA shows the error:

BP_DATAREPL_FND / 006

What is already configured:

  • Communication System created in S/4HANA with Business System field filled with the Display ID obtained from the default communication system in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2
  • Communication Arrangement SAP_COM_0008 active with the correct Communication System assigned
  • Additional Properties: Integration with C4C enabled = X and No Multiple Address Handling = X
  • Inbound Service active: Business Partner - Replicate from Client to SAP S/4HANA Cloud (SOAP)
  • Communication User linked to the Communication System and Communication Arrangement
  • iFlow Replicate Business Partner to SAP S4HANA Cloud deployed and running in SAP Cloud Integration

Question:
What could be causing the error BP_DATAREPL_FND/006 with all this configuration in place? Is there any additional configuration needed specifically for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to authorize the inbound replication coming from SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?

Any help or relevant SAP Notes would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: From reactive maintenance to autonomous agents
entry.content:
Unplanned equipment downtime can cost industrial companies tens or even hundreds of thousands of dollars per hour. In highly asset-intensive industries, losses can be even higher. This is not just a maintenance issue. It is a business issue that touches revenue, safety, compliance, and brand reputation.

Every business that depends on physical equipment, machines in a factory, turbines in a power plant, infrastructure in a warehouse, faces a fundamental question: how do you keep your operations running reliably without overspending on maintenance or exposing your workforce to unnecessary risk?

Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (SAP Cloud ERP) addresses exactly this challenge. It is a core ERP capability, built on decades of SAP expertise across asset-intensive industries, that goes far beyond a simple maintenance tool. In this post, I share insights from a recent conversation with Akif Ünal (Product Management) and Amar Kumar (Product Marketing) at SAP, where we explored what SAP delivers today, how real customers are transforming their operations, and what the future looks like with AI-powered agents.

Watch the full Expert Talk: 

Why asset management matters more than you think

Consider a simple analogy: owning a car. When you buy a car, your journey does not end at the dealership, it begins there. You perform regular oil changes, check tire pressure, listen for unusual sounds. You do all of this to ensure reliability, maintain performance, and protect the long-term value of that asset.

Now scale that up. Your company does not have a single car. It has a fleet of critical, high-value equipment: production lines, heavy machinery, essential infrastructure. Not managing them properly is not just an inconvenience, it directly impacts the bottom line.

The business case for asset management rests on three pillars:

Preventing unplanned breakdowns. When a critical machine fails unexpectedly, production stops, customer orders are delayed, and revenue is lost. Effective asset management moves you from costly reactive firefighting to planned, proactive maintenance, predicting and preventing failures before they happen.

Controlling total cost of ownership. Like with a car, the purchase price is only part of the story. Asset management provides the visibility to understand true maintenance costs, analyze spending patterns on recurring versus breakdown maintenance, and simulate future costs to create smarter budgets.

Making your teams faster and smarter. Today, planners and supervisors often navigate multiple systems to get a complete picture of an asset’s history and health. Modern asset management consolidates all that data into a single, intelligent view, empowering teams to identify recurring problems, optimize schedules, and make confident decisions.

For a company already running SAP, this is not about adding another system. They have now the possibility to add value of the critical assets you already own and turning what is often seen as a cost center into a driver of operational excellence.

What SAP Cloud ERP actually covers

Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is not merely a maintenance application. It is an integrated end-to-end solution embedded directly into the operational and financial backbone of the enterprise. Built on standardized master data, technical object structures, reusable configuration, and proven best-practice processes, SAP enables comprehensive asset management out of the box.

 

Asset Management capabilities overview in SAP Cloud ERP

Operationally, SAP supports the full spectrum of maintenance strategies:

Reactive maintenance handles breakdowns, emergency repairs, and fast-track corrective actions when failures occur. It is the safety net when something goes wrong despite your best efforts.

Proactive and preventive maintenance helps companies avoid failures altogether through planned strategies based on time intervals, operation hours, counters, measurement points, and condition monitoring.

Improvement maintenance covers larger engineering-driven activities such as plant upgrades, redesigns, and operational optimization initiatives—coordinated through the project system that controls all activities, tasks, and costs.

Operations and overhead maintenance addresses activities that are not part of typical maintenance tasks, such as training, documentation, and development work.

A key differentiator is the deep ERP integration. Asset management processes are directly connected with logistics, procurement, inventory management, and workforce-related processes in one harmonized cloud environment. This tight integration eliminates the silos that traditionally make asset management fragmented and expensive to maintain.

Real-world transformation: A gas company in the Middle East

To understand the tangible impact, consider a gas company in the Middle East—a joint venture of a global gas company giant operating in a challenging environment. Before their transformation, they ran a heavily customized legacy ERP system with custom-built solutions for asset management.

The pain was significant: their asset management solution was not integrated with the rest of the ERP processes. It was disconnected from logistics, procurement, and finance. They had built extensive custom integrations that brought higher operational costs, prevented them from staying current with SAP standards, and left them without access to modern functionality.

When they moved to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition the transformation was immediate and measurable. The modern Fiori-first user experience meant that every transaction was accessible through a simple web browser, a significant improvement for field teams. Standardized best-practice processes for reactive, proactive, and all other maintenance types replaced their fragmented custom solutions.

Most importantly, the cloud platform delivered real-time insights across asset management, finance, procurement, and logistics in a single environment. And because they are now on the cloud, every six months SAP delivers the latest innovations automatically. They no longer have to plan and execute expensive upgrade projects to benefit from new capabilities.

This example illustrates a pattern we see across industries: moving to a standardized cloud platform with integrated asset management brings not only operational efficiency but positions the organization for continuous, future-proof innovation.

The AI vision: From conversational AI to autonomous agents

While Joule provides a strong conversational entry point for AI in asset management, SAP’s vision is much larger. The goal is to fundamentally change how key personas in asset management work every day, moving toward what SAP calls the autonomous enterprise.

Consider the challenges these personas face today:

The maintenance supervisor is responsible for screening notifications and deciding which ones to accept or reject. In a world of connected equipment and IoT platforms, every anomaly raises a notification. When thousands of notifications arrive for the same issue, the triage work becomes overwhelming. AI can help by intelligently grouping, deduplicating, and prioritizing these notifications.

The maintenance planner faces even more complexity. They must coordinate in-house and external technicians, review maintenance history, control costs under constant pressure, and navigate multiple applications for the information they need. AI can synthesize fragmented data into a single on-demand summary of the asset and recommend optimal planning decisions.

The maintenance technician works in the field and needs the simplest possible way to report issues. Instead of manually creating notifications, a technician can take a photograph and ask the AI agent to create a notification of a defect, reducing administrative burden and speeding up the entire response chain.

The Operational Maintenance Planner Agent

The Operational Maintenance Planner Agent: from notification to execution-ready work order in minutes

One of the most compelling examples of this AI vision in action is the Operational Maintenance Planner Agent. This agent generates complete work order structures from approved notifications, recommending order types, operations, task lists, work centers, and durations based on context and maintenance history.

In practice, this works through a conversational interface. The planner asks the agent which notifications are ready for planning. The agent retrieves the list, provides a comprehensive summary of each notification, and offers options: search for reference orders from the past, or create a new work order. If the planner chooses to look at history, the system searches business data, both master data and transactional records, and returns matching reference orders with confidence scores.

The result: what previously required navigating four or five different applications and manually correlating information now happens in minutes through a natural, conversational flow. The human remains in the loop at every decision point, but the heavy lifting of data gathering, correlation, and recommendation is handled by the agent.

The agent also executes parallel readiness checks across all prerequisites, initiates required processes (such as work permits), and communicates with other agents for planning optimization. The outcome is a significant reduction in mean time to repair.

Looking ahead: The roadmap

AI Agent roadmap for Asset Management

The Operational Maintenance Planner Agent is just the beginning. SAP has a comprehensive pipeline of AI agents planned for asset management:

In the second half of 2026, expect the Functional Location Insight Agent, the Maintenance Request Initiation Agent, the Maintenance Request Screening Agent, and the Maintenance Activity Insight Agent. Moving into 2027, the Equipment Insight Agent, the Repair or Replace Recommendation Agent, the Maintenance Job Debrief Agent, and the Work Permit Recommendation Agent are on the roadmap.

The takeaway

Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is not just a maintenance tool. It is a business resilience platform that protects your assets, protects your workers, and helps you control costs with greater precision.

With AI, the shift is clear: you stop reacting to failures and start predicting them. The agents coming in the next releases will transform how supervisors, planners, and technicians work, reducing manual effort, accelerating decision-making, and ensuring that the right maintenance happens at the right time.

If you are already running Asset Management, now is the time to explore capabilities you may not have activated yet. The platform is evolving rapidly, and every six months brings new functionality that is automatically available to you.

Want to dive deeper? Watch the full SAP Expert Talk session:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fyx68fY2be0

You can also reach us at insides4@sap.com for questions or to share your feedback.

entry.title: Enhancing the Support for RISE on AWS & GCP - LogServ with Sentinel for SAP Part 5
entry.content:
Find your way to our central blog series entry here.

Co-authored by Martin Pankraz (SAP Security PM, Microsoft) & @krishnarajapantula 

Dear community,

The wait is over. Finally, RISE on AWS, RISE on GCP and customers on any platform can be protected with Microsoft Sentinel Solution for SAP with SAP LogServ. How is that different from RISE on Azure, you are asking?

Well, Azure customers have a native LogServ data pipeline with turn-on experience where SAP does the heavy lifting for you. Customers in other hyperscalers’ can still profit from Microsoft Sentinel for SAP integration using the python-based log forwarder provided by SAP ECS. It has a dedicated Microsoft Sentinel for SAP module.

Architecture Overview with RISE on AWS sample

 

Architecture Overview with RISE on GCP sample

 

How to integrate SAP LogServ with Microsoft Sentinel for SAP

  1. SAP RISE customers who subscribed to LogServ, should install SAP LogServ (RISE), S/4 HANA Cloud Private Edition from Microsoft Sentinel Content Hub. Use the Data Connectors screen to deploy the connector – either from classic Azure Portal or the new view on the Defender Portal.
    1. The connector deployment tries to create all resources in one go. Among them a Microsoft Entra ID app registration. In case the user doing the deployment has not enough rights, this process needs to be split up. Click the button anyways, which finalizes the creation of the Data Collection Endpoint and Data Collection Rule in the same resource group as your Log Analytics Workspace. 
    2. Take note of the fields generated. They will be required for the log forwarder config.
    3. If needed, in a second step create your app registration, supply a secret, and assign that Entra ID app id to the Data Collection rule with the role "Monitoring Metrics Publisher".

2. Once installed the customer should reach out to their: 

  1. ECS CDM or ECS TSM to share log forwarder onboarding details. CDM and TSM's can find Sentinel onboarding steps in LogServ SAP internal wiki.
  2. Put sap-logserv-sentinel-integration@service.microsoft.com in cc 
  3. Use the subject line: "SAP LogServ and Microsoft Sentinel - Activation" Please include your SAP RISE customer details in the email.

3. Request virtual network peering (AWS VPC, GCP VPC peering) if not yet deployed

4. Retrieve the LogServ connection details from the self-service section of the SAP ECS Security Portal.

 

 

5. Deploy the log forwarder provided by SAP:

  1. Deploy a virtual machine with line of sight to the LogServ assets and access to the peered private network.
  2. Install the python-based SAP ECS Logforwarder.
  3. Configure the log forwarder with the config details collected from ECS Security Portal. Use the Sentinel configuration section together with the values noted down in step 1a.

6. Use the SAP LogServ Insights workbook to verify successful log data ingest.

 

What next?

  • See the blog series for an architecture overview, and log type details.
  • Understand the agentless data connector for the application layer from this Microsoft Learn article.

Get Started Today 

Don't miss this opportunity to enhance your security posture with the powerful combination of SAP LogServ and Microsoft Sentinel Solution for SAP. Activate today and be among the first to experience the benefits. 

We look forward to your participation and to helping you incorporate your SAP RISE environments into your overall IT estate. 

Check blog part 2 to cherry pick the log types you need from LogServ for real-time threat protection and which ones should go into cost-efficient long-term storage on the Sentinel Data Lake, part 3 to craft your own detections, part 4 to understand the monitoring.

And finally, part 1 of the series to discover the analytic rules for the application layer powering Microsoft’s correlation engine.

Final Words

That’s a wrap🌯. You saw today how to onboard your SAP LogServ instance running RISE on AWS or GCP to Microsoft Sentinel for SAP. The approach is applicable to all other SAP ECS supported environments. You understand the difference to RISE on Azure deployments integrated with Microsoft Sentinel for SAP. Otherwise, go check again the intro paragraph.

Get started with your deployment today. See the art-of-the-possible for the agentic SOC on this video.

Which detections or analytic rules for RISE do you need most? Let me know in the comments or reach out directly.

Cheers Hemanth & Martin

entry.title: Decoupling transport from feature
entry.content:
Hello, 

As we know decoupling of transport from feature is not supported as of now, but if we have one transport which is wrongly captured in feature, how to decouple it from feature and move all other TRs to next system.

Thanks and Regards,

entry.title: Re: Cycle Counting next planned Counting
entry.content:
Dear @bastianD,

The standard solution is not a separate next counting date field in the Warehouse Monitor cycle counting view. The standard planning logic is handled when physical inventory documents are created or scheduled for cycle counting

The relevant process is to maintain the cycle counting indicator / interval for the product and then use Create Inventory Documents Cycle Counting or Schedule Inventory Document Creation Cycle Counting. During this step the system determines which products are due for cycle counting based on the cycle counting indicator interval the configured buffer time and the product’s last relevant inventory count date. The system then creates the physical inventory documents for the products that are due. The Count Date / Planned Count Date is used as the planned count date in the created physical inventory documents it is not shown as a separate next counting date field in the Warehouse Monitor view

More details below:

SAP Learning Checking Physical Inventory Levels

SAP Help Configuration for Create Inventory Documents - Cycle Counting

SAP Help How to Schedule the Creation of Physical Inventory Documents for Annual Inventory and Cycle Counting

SAP Help Manage Physical Inventory Documents

Additional information:

SAP confirms for Public Edition that cycle counting depends on the cycle counting indicator maintained in the product master. The KBA also mentions mass maintenance via Warehouse Monitor -> Product Master Data -> Warehouse Attribute -> More Method -> Mass Change/Create

More details below:

SAP KBA 3588454 - Cycle counting report is empty, No stock find for party entitled to dispose

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: MDK app button bound to key
entry.content:
AFAIK the SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) and SAP Mobile Services do not provide native functionality to directly bind app button actions to specific hardware key presses on Android scanners out of the box.

  • MDK supports metadata-driven app customization and rule binding for UI elements such as buttons.
  • There is no documented API or metadata configuration in the standard SAP Mobile Services MDK client for handling hardware key events (e.g., scanner keys) to trigger button rules.
  • While custom development in the native layer (e.g., a custom MDK client) could capture hardware key events and invoke MDK rules, this is not a supported feature in the standard SAP Mobile Services MDK client.
  • The official MDK API documentation and release notes do not reference hardware key-to-rule binding capabilities.
  • SAP Mobile Services MDK client updates primarily focus on bug fixes and general feature enhancements, with no explicit support for hardware key event handling.

Triggering MDK rules bound to UI buttons via hardware key presses on Android scanners is not officially supported as a configurable feature within SAP Mobile Services MDK. (at your own risk, no support)

entry.title: Re: Mass PO Closure blocked by “Release orders not possib...
entry.content:
Hello!

SAP KBA 3590976 confirms this error "Release orders against contract xxxxxx not possible (contract not released)”  as a known issue in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. It occurs when a PO references a contract that has not been formally released through the workflow approval process.

The cause is: in S/4HANA Public Cloud, the system re-validates the PO's source assignment (contract reference) upon any save operation, including when setting the Delivery Completed or Final Invoice indicators. The contract release check is not scoped to "creation of new release orders" only; it starts on any change to a PO item that references an unreleased contract. This means the system interprets closure action (e.g., setting ELIKZ = 'X') as a change to a document linked to an unvalidated source, and it hard-stops accordingly.

In S/4HANA Public Cloud (multi-tenant), the options are more restricted than in Private Cloud or On-Premise. What may be feasible via Custom Logic BAdI: using Modify Purchase Order Item to pre-set the Delivery Completed indicator automatically under certain conditions (e.g., zero outstanding GR, specific purchasing group), reducing manual effort even if some items still fail.

 

Practical workarounds for mass PO closure linked to unreleased contracts:

Option A: Batch contract release via workflow re-trigger (recommended for contract-blocked items). For PO items blocked by an unreleased contract (like 4610068068), the viable path is to trigger re-release on the contract: make a minor, reversible change to a non-material field (e.g., validity end date extended by one day), allow the auto-approval workflow to process it, then revert the change.

Option B: segment the mass closure by error type. Use the Mass Changes to Purchase Orders app, but pre-filter to exclude contract-linked items. The Mass Changes to Purchase Orders app supports delivery date filtering and batch field changes across multiple PO items. Process the non-contract-linked items (blocked supplier, header inconsistencies, etc.) in one pass, and handle contract-linked items separately after Option A. https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/s4hana-cloud-sourcing-procurement/manage-purchase-orders-faq

Option C: Deletion indicator as alternative to delivery completed. For closure where no further GR or invoice posting should occur, the deletion indicator is the alternative approach. Items flagged with the deletion indicator no longer appear in open PO reports like ME2N.

entry.title: Re: Guidance required for correcting PO price after Goods...
entry.content:
Hello!

Because stock is now zero, the price correction path depends on whether an invoice has already been posted or not. The scenarios are:

Scenario A. Invoice not yet posted against this GR

Step 1: Correct the PO Price First (in Manage Purchase Orders). SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition permits changes to the net price of a purchase order even after goods receipt has been posted. Navigate to the Manage Purchase Orders Fiori app, open the relevant PO, correct the unit price on the affected line item, and save. OSS Note: 3642802 - The system permits changes to the purchase order net price after the goods receipt has been posted

Step 2: Post the Supplier Invoice at the Correct Price (Create Supplier Invoice). Using the Create Supplier Invoice app, you can create a supplier invoice with reference to the purchase order, entering the correct price as per the actual supplier invoice. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/01060a2e758f4e8a9d4a7cf07b0ce323.html?locale=en-US

When the invoice is posted at the corrected price, the system will automatically calculate the difference between the GR value (posted at the old wrong price) and the invoice value (at the correct price). The accounting treatment depends on stock availability: if the quantity associated with the GR has already been delivered and the stock is zero, the system makes the offsetting entry to the consumption account (for PO items with account assignment) or to the price difference account (for standard-price-controlled materials), rather than the stock account. https://learning.sap.com/courses/invoice-verification-in-sap-s-4hana/entering-subsequent-debits-credits

 

Scenario B.  Invoice already posted at the wrong price

This is the more complex case and requires the Subsequent Debit or Subsequent Credit process. A subsequent debit is required when a PO item has already been invoiced and further costs are incurred, for example, when a vendor invoiced too low and sends a second invoice for the difference. A subsequent credit is required when a PO item was invoiced at too high a price and the vendor sends a credit memo for the difference. S

When entering a subsequent debit or credit, the system suggests the entire invoiced quantity but no value. The maximum quantity that can be subsequently debited or credited is the quantity already invoiced. A subsequent debit or credit can only be entered for a PO item if an invoice has already been posted for that item.

How to execute in S/4HANA Cloud: within the Create Supplier Invoice app (Manage Supplier Invoices), you can create a subsequent credit or a subsequent debit directly, in addition to standard invoices and credit memos.

Steps:

Step 1. Open the Create Supplier Invoices app. In the transaction type dropdown, select Subsequent Debit (if the original invoice was too low, price needs to go up) or Subsequent Credit (if the original invoice was too high, price needs to come down).

Step 2. Enter the PO number as the reference document. The system will propose the full invoiced quantity; enter only the value difference (do not re-enter quantity if you want the posting to go to price difference/consumption account only)

Step 3. Simulate the accounting entries before posting to confirm the GL accounts

Accounting entries generated (zero stock scenario): Since the GR quantity has already been delivered and stock is zero, the system makes the offsetting entry to the consumption account (for account-assigned PO items) or the price difference account, not the stock account.

 

Scenario C. moving average price (MAP) consideration

If Plant 5901 uses Moving Average Price (MAP / Price Control V) for the material: when stock was zero at the time of the original GR or invoice, the MAP cannot be updated through the stock account (since stock = 0). SAP posts the difference to the Price Difference account (PRD) instead. This is standard behavior and does not require any manual correction to the MAP,  the financial impact is correctly captured in PRD. The Manage Material Valuation (formerly MR21) app can be used to manually adjust the MAP.

 

Step-by-Step process summary for GRN 5000101730, Plant 5901:

Step 1  Confirm invoice status against GRN 5000101730

        - Check PO history in Manage Purchase Orders app

        - Determine if invoice already posted (Scenario A or B)

Step 2  If invoice NOT posted:

        - Correct PO price in Manage Purchase Orders

       -  Post supplier invoice at correct price via Create Supplier Invoice

       -  System auto-posts price difference to Consumption/PRD account

If invoice already posted:

       -  Go to Create Supplier Invoice app

        -  Select transaction type: Subsequent Credit (overcharged)

          or Subsequent Debit (undercharged)

        - Reference the PO number

        - Enter only the VALUE difference (not quantity)

        -  Simulate accounting entries → confirm consumption/PRD posting

Step 3  Finance verification

        - Confirm vendor liability is correctly stated

        - Confirm price difference / consumption GL entries are accurate

        - GR/IR clearing account should be balanced

Step 4  If payment already made at wrong amount:

        - Coordinate debit memo (if overpaid) or additional payment  (if underpaid) with the supplier

entry.title: Unable to Reverse Unplanned Depreciation Across Fiscal Years
entry.content:
Hi Team,For entity SE63, we cannot execute ordinary depreciation because unplanned depreciation was posted in the past. To continue processing depreciation correctly, those historical unplanned depreciation postings must first be reversed.

However, when attempting to reverse the posting, SAP does not allow the reversal because the original posting belongs to fiscal year 2024, while the reversal would be posted in fiscal year 2026.

entry.title: Re: Dynamic Fiscal Period Determination in EBRR period-end close run application jobs
entry.content:
Hi rchoc, thanks for the positive feedback — we’re glad you like the new capability.
At the moment, we delivered this enhancement specifically for Event‑Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR). It does not extend to the “Schedule Overhead Accounting Jobs (F3767)” app.
If you need similar dynamic period determination for overhead accounting jobs, please submit a Customer Influence (CIP) request as described in the SAP KBA document https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2963059.
In the request, kindly describe:
  • the feature that you would like to be enabled
  • mention Schedule Overhead Accounting Jobs 
  • describe requested behavior of the fiscal periods
This will help product management assess and prioritize the requirement.

entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors Integrations Beginners Guide- Exploring Learning Management System
entry.content:
Great blog! I can confirm that as of 2026, this approach still works well.

I just wanted to add a couple of notes that may help anyone having trouble obtaining the access token from the initial POST request.

First, make sure you're using your Learning instance URL rather than the BizX URL from the SuccessFactors platform. The easiest way I found to get the correct URL was to log in to the Learning Administration home page, copy your company instance URL from the browser, and append:

/learning/oauth-api/rest/v1/token

Also, be aware that your Company ID in Learning may be different from your BizX Company ID. This is important because you'll need to Base64-encode the Learning Company ID when creating the authorization header.

For example, in our environment the URL uses the Company ID shown on the Learning Admin OAuth Token Server page, followed by:

lms.sapsf.com/learning/admin

This matched the URL displayed in our browser and was the correct instance to use for generating the access token successfully.

Hopefully this saves someone else some troubleshooting time!
